subUnits.title: 12.1 Comprehensive Revision: Company Accounts, Capital Market, and Examination Techniques
notes: Comprehensive Revision: Company Accounts, Capital Market, and Examination Preparation
Key Concepts:
1. Company Accounts Revision:
a) Formation of Company:
- CAMA 1990/2020 requirements, Memorandum, Articles, Prospectus
- Private vs public companies: features and distinctions
b) Issue of Shares:
- Application, allotment, calls accounts
- Share premium and discount accounts
- Preference vs ordinary shares
c) Loan Capital:
- Debentures, mortgages, interest accounting
- Differences between shares and debentures
d) Final Accounts:
- Profit and Loss Appropriation Account: goodwill, preliminary expenses, reserves, dividends
- Balance Sheet: format, preparation, ratio analysis
2. Capital Market Revision:
a) Financial System Components:
- Money market vs capital market: features and instruments
- Key institutions: CBN, SEC, NSE, CSCS, FMDQ
b) Methods of Raising Funds:
- Offer for sale, offer for subscription, rights issues, private placement
- Requirements for accessing capital market in Nigeria
c) Benefits of Capital Market:
- To investors, government, economy, and companies
- Nigerian examples: NSE-listed companies, FGN bonds
d) Regulation of Capital Market:
- Statutory regulation (SEC) vs self-regulation (NSE)
- Regulatory tools: licensing, disclosure, enforcement
3. Examination Preparation Strategies:
a) Formula Mastery:
- Share issue calculations, ratio analysis, appropriation account entries
- Consistent units and clear working steps
b) Account Format Drawing:
- Use ruler for straight lines, label all columns clearly
- Include date, particulars, folio, debit, credit columns
c) Common WAEC/NECO Pitfalls:
- Confusing share premium with share capital
- Incorrect classification of assets/liabilities in Balance Sheet
- Omitting adjustments in appropriation account
d) Time Management:
- Allocate time per question based on marks
- Attempt high-mark questions first if confident
- Reserve time for review and unit checks
4. Nigerian Exam Standards:
- WAEC/NECO emphasize clear working, correct formats, and logical flow
- Practical questions require accurate calculations and proper account preparation
- Theory questions reward concise, precise answers with relevant formulas
Nigerian Context:
- Align with WAEC/NECO marking schemes and examiner reports
- Emphasize local applications: NSE listings, SEC regulations, CAMA compliance
- Practice calculation-heavy and concept-based questions from past papers
Conclusion:
- Master company accounts and capital market concepts with Nigerian context
- Practice past questions under timed conditions
- Focus on formats, adjustments, and regulatory compliance
- Improve accuracy, clarity, and exam readiness

Diagram of capital market regulatory framework
Sample past questions with model solutions and marking schemes
Nigerian context: WAEC/NECO standards and local corporate examples

quiz.q: In share issue accounts, application money is recorded in:
quiz.explanation: Application money is first recorded in Share Application Account before allotment.

quiz.q: Share premium account is created when shares are issued:
quiz.explanation: Share premium account records excess amount received over nominal value when shares are issued above face value.

quiz.q: In Balance Sheet, fixed assets are shown at:
opts: Net book value (cost less accumulated depreciation)
quiz.explanation: Fixed assets are shown at net book value (cost less accumulated depreciation) in Balance Sheet.

quiz.q: Profit and Loss Appropriation Account shows:
quiz.explanation: Appropriation Account shows how net profit is distributed among reserves, dividends, and retained earnings.

quiz.q: In Nigerian capital market, SEC stands for:
quiz.explanation: Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is the principal regulator of Nigerian capital market.

quiz.q: A rights issue protects existing shareholders by:
opts: Giving them first option to buy new shares
quiz.explanation: Rights issues give existing shareholders proportional right to buy new shares, preventing ownership dilution.

quiz.q: A common error in Balance Sheet preparation is:
opts: Including current liabilities in non-current section
quiz.explanation: Misclassifying current liabilities as non-current (or vice versa) causes errors in financial position presentation.

quiz.q: To prepare for WAEC/NECO Financial Accounting exams, you should:
opts: Practice past questions with timed conditions
quiz.explanation: Timed past question practice builds familiarity, speed, and exam readiness for WAEC/NECO.
Debenture holders are creditors who receive fixed interest and have priority in repayment over shareholders.

quiz.q: In Nigerian context, capital market benefits include:
opts: Long-term wealth creation, infrastructure financing, and economic growth
quiz.explanation: Nigerian capital market supports long-term wealth creation, infrastructure financing through bonds, and overall economic growth.
